evidence_quotes.quote: This is the reality for most online video games.
no vendor should have unilateral power over a customer's access to any digital good, or good that requires access to an external online service.
The very core function of purchasing - the transfer of ownership - is self-evident for physical goods, but is very often diluted or even absent for digital goods.

paragraphs.text: Hi, It would be great if the Act could include stipulations regarding the protections of digital purchases. Currently, digital purchases by and large lack the protections that physical purchases do. Imagine if you bought a film on Blu-ray, and then, after some time, a person from the studio that owns the film knocked on your door and said "Sorry, but the studio has decided this film isn't available anymore" and smashed your disc to pieces. This is the reality for digital purchases of film, music, and apps. Imagine if you bought a car, and then, after some time, a person from the car manufacturer knocked on your door and said "Sorry, but we've decided we don't want to support this type of car anymore" and disabled the ignition so that the car couldn't ever start again. This is the reality for most online video games. Maybe these examples seem ridiculous, but it's no exaggeration that at present vendors of digital and online-dependent goods can get away with conditions that would seem completely absurd and obviously unfair if applied to physical goods. Online commerce has been around for decades now but still little has been done to ensure consumer protections in the space. The very core function of purchasing - the transfer of ownership - is self-evident for physical goods, but is very often diluted or even absent for digital goods. Put plainly: no vendor should have unilateral power over a customer's access to any digital good, or good that requires access to an external online service. An important note here is that flattening this power structure does not mean incurring undue or unreasonable costs for the producers, nor the impossibility of protecting proprietary technology or IP. It would simply require minor changes in design philosophy, such as ensuring the product can operate without an internet connection, and allowing peer-to-peer networking to replace central server networking after sunsetting a product. I hope you'll consider debating digital ownership for this Act and for future Acts also.
